)

FROM counts
WHERE cnt >= (SELECT AVG(cnt) FROM counts)
ORDER BY cnt DESC, dept;

  (5, 'Cyd', 50),
  (6, 'Cyd', 50);
-- global avg = (100+100+200+200+50+50)/6 = 116.67; Ava avg=100, Ben avg=200, Cyd avg=50
-- Only Ben qualifies (200 > 116.67), Ava is below

tests.name: Single customer all orders
All same amount empty result
Customer avg above via low and high orders
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER, customer TEXT, amount REAL);
INSERT INTO orders (id, customer, amount) VALUES
  (1, 'Ava', 10),
  (2, 'Ava', 490),
  (3, 'Ben', 50),
  (4, 'Ben', 60),
  (5, 'Cyd', 70),
  (6, 'Cyd', 80);
-- global avg = (10+490+50+60+70+80)/6 = 760/6 = 126.67
-- Ava avg = (10+490)/2 = 250 > 126.67 (qualifies)
-- Ben avg = (50+60)/2 = 55 < 126.67 (no)
-- Cyd avg = (70+80)/2 = 75 < 126.67 (no)
-- Trap: someone filtering WHERE amount > global_avg would INCLUDE 490 for Ava but exclude 10, giving wrong result

trap.sql:
SELECT customer, ROUND(AVG(amount), 2) AS cust_avg
FROM orders
WHERE amount > (SELECT AVG(amount) FROM orders)
GROUP BY customer
ORDER BY cust_avg DESC;
trap.note: Filters individual order rows by global avg instead of comparing per-customer averages to global avg; misidentifies customers when some orders are above and some below the global average.

tests.name: Born after ref date
Leap day birthday
Age zero born on ref date
Varying months same birth year

trap.sql:
SELECT name, birthday,
       CAST(strftime('%Y', '2024-06-15') AS INTEGER) - CAST(strftime('%Y', birthday) AS INTEGER)

FROM people ORDER BY name;
trap.note: Uses year subtraction with month-day adjustment instead of the YYYYMMDD integer trick; gives -1 (not 0) for anyone born after the reference date because integer division in SQLite truncates toward zero while year subtraction floors.

LEFT JOIN memberships m ON m.student_id = s.id

SELECT NULL AS student, m.club AS club

trap.sql:
WITH level0 AS (
  SELECT id, name, name AS path FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
),
level1 AS (
  SELECT e.id, e.name, l.path || ' > ' || e.name AS path
  FROM employees e JOIN level0 l ON e.manager_id = l.id
),
level2 AS (

  FROM employees e JOIN level1 l ON e.manager_id = l.id

level3 AS (

  FROM employees e JOIN level2 l ON e.manager_id = l.id

  UNION ALL SELECT id, name, path FROM level2
  UNION ALL SELECT id, name, path FROM level3

trap.sql:
WITH numbered AS (
  SELECT login_date,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY login_date) AS rn
  FROM logins
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT login_date,
         date(login_date, '-' || rn || ' days') AS grp
  FROM numbered
)
SELECT MIN(login_date) AS start_date,
       MAX(login_date) AS end_date,
       COUNT(*)        AS days
FROM grouped
GROUP BY grp
ORDER BY start_date;

tests.name: Single tag, no comma
Two tags
Five tags
Tags with spaces in values

trap.sql:
WITH RECURSIVE split(pos, item, rest) AS (
  SELECT
    1,
    SUBSTR(tags, 1, INSTR(tags, ',') - 1),
    SUBSTR(tags, INSTR(tags, ',') + 1)
  FROM posts WHERE id = 1 AND INSTR(tags, ',') > 0
  UNION ALL
  SELECT
    pos + 1,
    CASE WHEN INSTR(rest, ',') = 0 THEN rest
         ELSE SUBSTR(rest, 1, INSTR(rest, ',') - 1) END,
    CASE WHEN INSTR(rest, ',') = 0 THEN ''
         ELSE SUBSTR(rest, INSTR(rest, ',') + 1) END
  FROM split
  WHERE rest <> ''
)
SELECT pos AS position, item AS tag
FROM split
ORDER BY pos;
trap.note: The anchor row filters with INSTR(tags, ',') > 0, so a post with only one tag (no comma) returns no rows instead of the single tag at position 1.

tests.name: Mixed-case tags expose case-sensitive sort
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE post_tags (post_id INTEGER, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO post_tags (post_id, name) VALUES
  (1, 'Zebra'),
  (1, 'apple'),
  (1, 'Mango'),
  (2, 'SQL'),
  (2, 'data'),
  (2, 'DB');

trap.sql:
SELECT post_id, group_concat(name, ',') AS tags
FROM (SELECT post_id, name FROM post_tags ORDER BY post_id, name COLLATE NOCASE)
GROUP BY post_id
ORDER BY post_id;
trap.note: Uses COLLATE NOCASE for sorting, which matches case-insensitive alphabetical order but differs from the oracle's default case-sensitive ordering when tags contain mixed-case names (uppercase letters sort before lowercase in SQLite's default collation).

tests.name: Single row
Two rows only
All same amount
Fractional averages expose wrong rounding
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE sales (day TEXT, amount INTEGER);
INSERT INTO sales (day, amount) VALUES
  ('2024-01-01', 10),
  ('2024-01-02', 20),
  ('2024-01-03', 31),
  ('2024-01-04', 40),
  ('2024-01-05', 50);

trap.note: Rounds to 0 decimal places instead of 2; passes when all 3-row window averages happen to be whole numbers (as in the example with amounts 10,20,30,40,50,60) but truncates fractional averages like 20.33 to 20 when amounts don't divide evenly.

tests.name: Tie at rank 1 across multiple categories
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE sales (category TEXT, product TEXT, revenue INTEGER);
INSERT INTO sales (category, product, revenue) VALUES
  ('Food', 'Bread', 500),
  ('Food', 'Milk', 500),
  ('Food', 'Eggs', 200),
  ('Gear', 'Tent', 800),
  ('Gear', 'Rope', 800),
  ('Gear', 'Axe', 300);

trap.sql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT category, product, revenue,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY category ORDER BY revenue DESC) AS rk
  FROM sales
)
SELECT category, product, revenue
FROM ranked
WHERE rk <= 2
ORDER BY category, revenue DESC;
trap.note: Uses RANK() instead of ROW_NUMBER(): on the example data there are no ties so both produce identical results, but when two products share rank-2 revenue RANK() returns both while ROW_NUMBER() returns exactly one — exposing the tie-at-rank-2 edge case.

INSERT INTO players (player, points) VALUES
  ('Fay',  100),
  ('Gil',   90),
  ('Hal',   90),
  ('Ivy',   80),
  ('Jay',   70);

trap.sql:
SELECT player, points, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY points DESC) AS rnk
FROM players
ORDER BY rnk, player
LIMIT 3;
trap.note: Uses LIMIT 3 instead of WHERE rnk <= 3 — returns exactly 3 rows even when more players are tied at rank 3 (or rank 2 with a tie causing rank 3 to jump).

tests.name: Duplicate unit values in same store-quarter expose SUM DISTINCT trap
Store with missing quarters yields NULL not zero
Single store all four quarters one product each
Many stores with heavy multi-product per quarter aggregation

trap.sql:
SELECT store,
       SUM(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q1' THEN units END) AS q1,
       SUM(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q2' THEN units END) AS q2,
       SUM(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q3' THEN units END) AS q3,
       SUM(DISTINCT CASE WHEN quarter = 'Q4' THEN units END) AS q4
FROM sales2d
GROUP BY store
ORDER BY store;
trap.note: Uses SUM(DISTINCT ...) instead of SUM — silently deduplicates equal unit values, giving wrong totals when two products in the same store-quarter happen to have identical unit counts.

tests.name: Single student
NULL score
Identical score rows expose UNION dedup
Many students alphabetical order

trap.sql:
SELECT student, 'math' AS subject, math AS score FROM report
UNION
SELECT student, 'science' AS subject, science AS score FROM report
UNION
SELECT student, 'english' AS subject, english AS score FROM report
ORDER BY student, subject;
trap.note: Uses UNION instead of UNION ALL — silently deduplicates rows that are identical (e.g. two students with the same name and same scores), returning fewer rows than expected.

INSERT INTO weather VALUES
  (1, '2024-07-01', 10),
  (2, '2024-07-02', 15),
  (3, '2024-07-03', 20),
  (4, '2024-07-04', 25),
  (5, '2024-07-05', 30);

trap.sql:
SELECT today.recorded_on AS recorded_on
FROM weather today
JOIN weather yest
  ON yest.recorded_on = date(today.recorded_on, '-1 day')
WHERE today.temperature >= yest.temperature
ORDER BY recorded_on;
trap.note: Uses >= instead of > so days with equal temperature to the prior day are incorrectly included as 'warmer'.

tests.name: IDs out of date order — exposes ORDER BY id trap
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE events (id INTEGER, event_date TEXT);
INSERT INTO events (id, event_date) VALUES
  (10, '2024-01-06'),
  (3,  '2024-01-13'),
  (7,  '2024-01-20'),
  (1,  '2024-01-07'),
  (5,  '2024-01-14');

trap.sql:
SELECT id, event_date,
       strftime('%w', event_date) AS wday
FROM events
WHERE strftime('%w', event_date) IN ('0', '6')
ORDER BY id;
trap.note: Orders by id instead of event_date; matches the example because ids happen to be in chronological order, but fails when ids are inserted out of date order.

tests.name: Repeating decimal requires ROUND
Heavy credits flip ordering vs plain average
Single-course student
GPA tie sorted by student name
tests.setupSql:
CREATE TABLE enrollments (student TEXT, course TEXT, credits INTEGER, grade_point REAL);
INSERT INTO enrollments (student, course, credits, grade_point) VALUES
  ('Zara', 'Math',    2, 4.0),
  ('Zara', 'Art',     2, 2.0),
  ('Anna', 'Science', 2, 4.0),
  ('Anna', 'PE',      2, 2.0),
  ('Mark', 'History', 2, 1.0);

trap.sql:
SELECT student,
       SUM(credits * grade_point) / SUM(credits) AS gpa
FROM enrollments
GROUP BY student
ORDER BY gpa DESC, student;
trap.note: Omits ROUND so the gpa column is a raw floating-point; matches the example (all results happen to be exact decimals) but diverges when the weighted average is a repeating decimal like 10/3.
